Is AI Getting Quietly Dumber? AIStupidLevel: A 24-Hour Watchdog for LLM Degradation

This post walks through AIStupidLevel — a third-party benchmark site that keeps a 24-hour eye on 21 production AI models across 7 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, DeepSeek, Kimi, GLM). It throws coding, reasoning, and tool-calling tasks at them every few hours, runs each task 5 times to take the median with a 95% confidence interval, and uses the CUSUM change-point detection algorithm to catch when a model is quietly getting dumber. We'll cover why models degrade (silent updates, quantization), how the four test suites work, how to read the Stupid Meter, and the bundled Smart Router that auto-picks the best model for you.
Decoding AI Spec Sheets: Token, Context Window, Compute — What Are These Numbers Actually About?

Every time you check out an AI product's intro page, you see things like Token, Context Window, hundreds of billions of parameters, $X per million Tokens — but what do these numbers actually mean? This post breaks down Token, Context Window, compute, parameters, Temperature, and all the other jargon AI vendors love to use, so you don't get lost in marketing numbers anymore.
Want to Run Your Own LLM? Understanding Model Parameters and Quantization

If you've ever tried downloading an AI model to run on your own machine, you've probably hit Hugging Face and seen 7B, Q4_K_M, GGUF — a wall of codes with no clue which one your computer can handle. This post unpacks what those codes actually mean and how to pick a model that fits your hardware.
Intro to Generative AI and Machine Learning (2) — The Key Technique Behind AI Agents: Context Engineering

Today's AI applications are basically all tied to Prompt Engineering and Context Engineering. So what's the difference between the two, and how do they relate? This post digs into how these two concepts shape how AI Agents work.
Intro to Generative AI and Machine Learning (1) — It's All Just Text Prediction

AI has moved insanely fast over the past few years — to the point where even regular folks can get AI to help them do things, with Vibe Coding being a great example. Hung-yi Lee at NTU is running a Generative AI and Machine Learning course in 2025, so this post is my notes and takeaways from it.
Build Your Own AI Language Model Lab — No Help Needed, with LM Studio

Most of us have been bombarded by AI, ChatGPT, and other large language models for a while now, and we've all been using web services like ChatGPT or Claude.AI. The upside of these services is that they're easy to start with and convenient. The downsides? You need an internet connection, there are data privacy concerns, and there are API costs. So is there a way to self-host AI locally? Yep — and this post introduces a really handy tool for that called LM Studio.
Heard of LLM, RAG, Prompt? A Quick Guide to Common Generative AI Terms

Generative AI isn't just engineer territory anymore — it's seeping into our daily lives. If you use ChatGPT, Grok, Midjourney, or similar tools, these technical terms will start showing up in your world. If you're an engineer, I'd highly recommend understanding them — future development will lean even more on these technologies to build better products.
